title: Project Final Demo Prep
subtitle: Polish, Practise, Present — Show the World What You Built!
overview: Students prepare their capstone projects for the final demonstration. This session focuses on polishing the code, fixing last-minute bugs, writing a clear project description, and practising how to confidently present and explain their work to an audience. Students learn that how you present is just as important as what you build.

images._note: Add photos from the actual session — students rehearsing, polishing UI, peer feedback moments, demo run-throughs.

keyTopics: Demo-Ready Checklist — Does your app run without crashing?
Polishing the UI — Clean layout, readable fonts, good colours
Code Clean-Up — Remove test prints, add comments, organise functions
Writing a Project Description — Title, Purpose, Features, Tech Used
Presentation Structure — What, Why, How, Show It, What I Learned
Handling Questions — How to respond confidently when you don't know
Peer Feedback — Giving and receiving constructive criticism
Stretch Features — Last-minute additions that add wow factor
Rehearsal — Practise your demo at least twice before presenting
Mindset — Celebrate what you built, not just what works perfectly

activity.title: Demo Prep — Polish, Practise, Present
steps: Run your project from scratch — does it open and work without errors?
Go through the demo-ready checklist — fix anything that fails
Polish your UI — check colours, font sizes, button labels and spacing
Clean up your code — remove unused print() statements, add comments
Write your project description: Title, Purpose, Features, Tech Used
Practise your 2-minute demo speech — What, Why, How, Show It, What I Learned
Swap with a partner — give each other one praise and one suggestion
Implement your partner's best feedback if time allows
Rehearse your full demo one more time — out loud, not just in your head!
Final check — save your file, test one last time, you are demo-ready!

quiz.q: What is the FIRST thing you should do when preparing for a project demo?
options: Design a new logo for your app
Run your project from scratch and make sure it works without errors
Write new features you haven't tested yet
Delete all your comments to make code shorter
quiz.explanation: Always run your full project first! Make sure it opens, works end-to-end and doesn't crash before you do anything else. A working demo is more impressive than a feature-heavy broken one.

quiz.q: Which of these is part of a good project presentation structure?
options: Read out every line of code to the audience
Only show the final screen and say nothing
What it is, Why you built it, How it works, Live demo, What you learned
Apologise for everything that doesn't work
quiz.explanation: A great demo follows: What (project name and purpose) → Why (problem it solves) → How (tech used) → Show It (live demo) → What I Learned. This tells a complete story!

quiz.q: Why should you remove extra print() statements before your demo?
options: print() statements slow down Python
They clutter the terminal and look messy during a live presentation
print() doesn't work in Tkinter projects
The audience cannot see the terminal
quiz.explanation: Test print() statements left in your code make the terminal look messy during a demo. Clean code shows professionalism — keep only prints that are part of your app's actual output.

options: Cleaning your computer screen
Rewriting all your code from scratch
Improving the visual appearance — better colours, readable fonts, neat layout
Adding as many widgets as possible
quiz.explanation: Polishing the UI means making it look clean and professional — consistent colours, readable font sizes, proper spacing and clear button labels. First impressions matter in a demo!

quiz.q: What is a 'demo-ready checklist' used for?
options: A shopping list for computer parts
A list of all your code files
A step-by-step list to verify your project is fully prepared for presentation
A list of bugs you plan to fix later
quiz.explanation: A demo-ready checklist helps you systematically verify everything — does it run, does the UI look good, are errors handled, is your speech ready? It prevents last-minute surprises!

quiz.q: A classmate says your button labels are confusing. This feedback is:
options: Rude and should be ignored
Constructive — it helps you improve your project before the real demo
Irrelevant — UI doesn't matter
Wrong — buttons are always clear
quiz.explanation: Constructive feedback is a gift! If someone finds your UI confusing, your real audience will too. Use peer feedback to improve before the final demo — that's exactly what rehearsal is for.

quiz.q: How long should a typical student project demo be?
options: 30 seconds — just show the final screen
20 minutes — explain every line of code
1–3 minutes — clear, focused and to the point
As long as possible to show hard work
quiz.explanation: 1–3 minutes is the sweet spot for a student demo! Long enough to cover what, why, how and a live run — short enough to keep the audience engaged. Practise to fit your time!

quiz.q: What should you do if your app crashes during the live demo?
options: Panic and sit down
Say nothing and pretend it didn't happen
Calmly acknowledge it, explain what likely caused it, and show what does work
Blame the computer
quiz.explanation: Stay calm! Acknowledge the issue confidently: 'That's a known bug I'm working on — let me show you the parts that work.' Handling problems gracefully shows maturity and real coding experience.

quiz.q: Why is it important to practise your demo OUT LOUD before presenting?
options: It charges the laptop battery
It helps you memorise every line of code
It reveals timing issues, awkward pauses and parts where you get confused
It is not important — just wing it on the day
quiz.explanation: Practising out loud reveals things silent practice misses — you might stumble on explanations, run over time, or realise you can't explain a feature clearly. Rehearse at least twice!

quiz.q: What should your project description include?
options: Your favourite food and hobby
A list of every variable in your code
Project title, purpose, key features and technology used
The date you started coding and your teacher's name
quiz.explanation: A good project description covers: Title (what it's called), Purpose (what problem it solves), Features (what it can do), and Tech Used (Python, Tkinter, etc). Clear and concise!

quiz.q: What is the best response when an audience member asks a question you don't know the answer to?
options: Make up an answer confidently
Say 'I don't know' and walk away
Honestly say you're not sure but you'll find out — and explain what you do know
Tell them the question is not relevant
quiz.explanation: Honesty is always best! 'That's a great question — I'm not sure about that yet, but I'd like to find out' shows maturity and curiosity. No one expects you to know everything!

quiz.q: What is the purpose of adding CODE COMMENTS before your demo?
options: Comments make the code run faster
They help others (and future-you) understand what each part of the code does
Comments are required by Tkinter
They change how the GUI looks
quiz.explanation: Code comments explain your thinking! They show the audience you understand your code and help anyone reading it later. Good comments are a sign of a thoughtful developer.

quiz.q: Which of these is a STRETCH feature that could add wow factor to a Tkinter project?
options: Removing all buttons
Using only black and white colours
Adding a colour theme, custom fonts, or saving data to a file
Making the window as small as possible
quiz.explanation: Stretch features like colour themes, custom fonts, data saving or keyboard shortcuts make your project stand out! They show you went beyond the basics and explored independently.

quiz.q: Why should you celebrate what you built even if it's not perfect?
options: Because perfect code doesn't exist
Because the teacher will give marks for confidence only
Because building something from nothing is a real achievement — imperfect progress beats perfect inaction
Because nobody will notice the bugs
quiz.explanation: Every working project — however simple — represents real learning and effort. Professional developers ship imperfect products too! Celebrate your progress, document what you'd improve, and keep building.

quiz.q: What is the LAST thing you should do before your demo presentation?
options: Add a brand new untested feature
Rewrite your entire project in a new language
Save your file and do one final full test run
Delete your backup copy to save space
quiz.explanation: Always save and do one final test run! This confirms everything still works after your last edits. Never add untested features right before a demo — it's a recipe for unexpected crashes!
